coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots in catalysis: Synthesis, properties, applications, comparative advantages, and future directions
coredata.dc:creator: Goyal, Sandeep
Chaudhary, Savita
Umar, Ahmad
Ibrahim, Ahmed A.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), since invention holds sententious potential because of their multifaceted optoelectronic characteristics, superior biocompatibility, phenomenal photoluminescence abilities. Their synthesis from readily available precursors, combined with easy functionalization, has categorized them as effective material in energy storage, fluorescent sensing and bio-imaging applications. The advanced synthetic modifications via doping and surface functionalization have further enhanced their catalytic efficiencies in environmental remediation application via finding sustainable solution for degrading harmful pollutants. The current review explore the significant amelioration during the synthesis of CDs, their characterization and role in catalysis, highlighting their mechanistic role in photo degradation, with particular focus in addressing environmental challenges. A comparative analysis of CDs with conventional metal based nano-catalysts has also been explored in term of stability, environmental compatibility, and cost-effectiveness. The review concludes via discussing probable challenges in reproducibility, large-scale production, and discussing upcoming research potential for amplifying their prospective outlooks.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), since invention holds sententious potential because of their multifaceted optoelectronic characteristics, superior biocompatibility, phenomenal photoluminescence abilities. Their synthesis from readily available precursors, combined with easy functionalization, has categorized them as effective material in energy storage, fluorescent sensing and bio-imaging applications. The advanced synthetic modifications via doping and surface functionalization have further enhanced their catalytic efficiencies in environmental remediation application via finding sustainable solution for degrading harmful pollutants. The current review explore the significant amelioration during the synthesis of CDs, their characterization and role in catalysis, highlighting their mechanistic role in photo degradation, with particular focus in addressing environmental challenges. A comparative analysis of CDs with conventional metal based nano-catalysts has also been explored in term of stability, environmental compatibility, and cost-effectiveness. The review concludes via discussing probable challenges in reproducibility, large-scale production, and discussing upcoming research potential for amplifying their prospective outlooks.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: From the time of their discoveries in 2004, carbon dots (CDs) exhibits a revolutionary place among nanomaterials [1]. On the basis of dimensionalities, CDs belongs to the class of carbon based zero dimensional nanoparticles having size less than 10 nm [2]. Furthermore, CDs are categorized into different subgroups including graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dot (CQDs), and carbon nano dots (CNDs) [3]. The crystalline structure having conjugated π graphene sheets, with lateral dimensions less than 20 nm with quantum confinement effect has been observed in case of GQDs [4]. The existing graphene lattices along with the chemical functional groups on the edges and interlayer defects have also produced significant impact on the physicochemical properties of GQDs. However, spherical nanodots with crystalline behaviour and quasi spherical amorphous aptitude have been observed in case of CQDs and CNDs respectively [5]. Additionally, the quantum confinement effect dependent on their size has been observed in CQDs, whereas, no quantum confinement effect has been observed in case of CNDs [6] ( Fig. 1). The superior extent of carbonization has also been evident in case of CNDs in comparison to CQDs. The photoluminescence ability of CNDs is mainly originated due to the defect states in the core and the surface [7]. Being a potent luminescent materials to effective catalyst, CDs possessed significant potential in drug delivery, sensing, adsorption, energy storage devices and many more [8]. CDs have also possessed the electron accepting and donating tendency via excitation with light of suitable wavelength [9]. Along with luminescence properties, CDs have acquired the photo induced potential abilities with electron well effect. The usage of CDs in batteries and energy storage devices has also been explored by researchers [10]. CDs showed good electrical conductivity which facilitates efficient electron transfer during electrochemical reactions which makes them suitable electro catalyst and an efficient material in fuel cell to enhance oxygen reduction reaction [11,12]. These advanced properties of CDs transformed them into a superior catalyst where it enhance efficiency, reaction rate and selectivity and successfully replaced traditional catalyst having toxicity, high cost and lower efficiencies [13]. On comparison with other conventional metal based catalyst, CDs are more efficient and environment friendly because of their ease in preparation from natural and bio-waste based precursors (Fig. 1).

The catalytic potential of CDs created the revolution in the field of waste water management and organic transformations [14]. CDs are useful in generating clean hydrogen fuel and reducing the carbon footprints [15]. CDs also exhibits a significant role towards the synthesis of organic compounds in various reactions such as oxidation, reduction and C-C coupling, esterification and cyclo-addition reactions [16]. CDs can be applied to light-driven catalytic processes, like water splitting and the breakdown of organic contaminants [17].

Up to now, various ranges of comprehensive reviews have discussed the synthesis, characteristic properties and potential optical and photoluminescence abilities of CDs from different outlooks. In reference to the catalytic prospective, the impact of synthetic methodologies, precursor source, effect of size and shape has partially been explored so far. In contrast to the existing review of CDs, which mainly explored on the fluorescence abilities, our consolidated data in this review displayed higher priorities on the fabrication methodologies- characteristic properties – performance abilities in reference to catalytic and photo-luminesce abilities. Our extensive research work on CDs has further supported our superior chance to explore the catalytic efficiencies of CDs in the field of waste water management. The consolidated review here provides a better outlook to discover the contrasting aptitude of CDs to the overall performance in catalysis in reference to the conventionally used catalysts and hereby opened new opportunities for the CDs based applications in different arenas. Additionally, the consolidated reports on the effect of dopants and surface modifications on catalytic efficiencies of CDs plays a critical role in interpreting the internal structure–potential application relationships in CDs, which further widens our comprehensive knowledge towards CDs. Thus the central aim of this review is to highlight the revolutionary effect of CDs in catalysis by providing the overview analysis of their mechanism, applications and future challenges and perspective.

Due to the significant properties of CDs, diverse ranges of methodologies were established so far for the synthesis of CDs ( Fig. 2). However, researchers around the globe are keen to establish novel, easy, quick, economical and easily scalable methodologies to produce CDs. The chosen methodologies have direct influenced on the size, internal composition and structural properties of CDs. Broadly, the existing synthetic approaches are categorized into top-down and bottom up strategies. The conversion of large sized carbon based materials such as carbon nanotube, graphite, graphene, and soot into CDs are mainly involved in top-down down approach. On the other hand, bottom up approach includes the conversion of small molecule into CDs by employing various strategies such as hydrothermal, microwave assistant, thermal decomposition [18]. The advantages such as control over the size and shape, scalability, versatility make it a suitable approach towards the synthesis of CDs. The chosen methodologies have possessed direct impact on tailoring the physicochemical properties of CDs in reference to their specific applications in different areas.
This segment gives an outline of recent improvements in the fabrication methodologies used for the synthesis of CDs using top-down and bottom up approaches.

The arc discharge technique is considered as one of the easiest and widely used methodologies for preparing CDs. Usually, the inert gases such as argon or helium has employed in gas chamber. The synthesis of CDs has been done over the carbon electrode placed inside the gas chamber. The high voltage has been usually employed across the electrode to generate high heat. This high temperature has the ability to vaporize the chosen precursor’s source from the anode. Afterwards the vaporized carbon allow to cools to obtain CDs [19]. This method is quite simple and large quantity of CDs can be easily prepared with this procedure. However, the obtained CDs were found to be less pure and possessed lower control over the size and properties of CDs [20]. Initially, Xu et al. have employed this arc discharge methodology to synthesize CDs by using single wall carbon nanotube by oxidation between arc discharge soot and nitric acid [21]. Wang et al. synthesized [22] CDs like graphene nano flakes by methane decomposition in pressure range 20–200 kPa by arc discharged method. The particles showed spherical shape with average particle size 3–8 nm. Fig. 3a represented the pictorial representation of formation of CDs like graphene flakes by using arc discharge method. Usually, the requirement of high temperature and lesser control over the size and non-reproducible results have now insisted the researchers to use better alternative techniques to generate CDs using other methods.

Here laser beams of high energy source has been employed for generating CDs. The mechanism involves the targeting of carbon precursor source either in liquid or vapour phase via using high power laser [23]. Consequently, the targeted molecule ablates into solvent and finally broken down smaller sized particles (Fig. 3b). The vaporized particles rapidly cools and CDs are prepared after condensation [23]. The synthesized CDs have also been purified by methods such as filtration, centrifugation and dialysis. This method is quite fast and generates CDs in pure form. For instance, Cutroneo et al. synthesized charcoal derived CDs in phosphate buffer solution by using laser ablation method by irradiation for 30 min with pulse IR diode laser [24]. Cui et al. employed dual beam pulse ablation to prepare CDs by using low cost carbon cloth with quantum yield 35 %. The synthesized CDs have possessed excellent anti jamming performance and good stability and high photo luminescent properties. The developed properties make them suitable for cell bio imaging [25]. Leon et al. synthesized nitrogen doped graphene CDs by using pulse ablation in liquid by using Nd: YAG laser (532 nm). The average size of obtained CDs was 3 nm with spherical shape. The developed particles displayed high PL properties which further modified into on-off-on photoluminescence probe for selective detection of ascorbic acid [26]. In a separate report, Cui et al. developed homogeneous CDs by using ethyl acetate and dimethylsulfoxide as precursor source in presence of ultrafast dual-beam pulsed laser ablation process [27]. Cortes et al. [28] employed laser ablation method for preparation of CDs and designed a glucose sensor by implementing CDs with 3-aminophenylboronic acid. Fig. 4b illustrates that, the preparation and modification of turn of fluorescent CDs sensor in non-enzymatic glucose detection. The topology of the formed particles has displayed significant variation in reference to the chosen laser source. However, the low quantum yield, high equipment cost makes it less favourable as compared to other methods.

However, researchers are still exploring the fabrication of CDs with controlled size and properties. The usage of preferred starting precursors has possessed significant impact on the structural properties of prepared CDs. It has further been established that the application of carbonaceous and polymer precursors have displayed the promoting effect on the optical properties of CDs. The application of biomass based precursors have supported the biocompatibility and cost managed synthesis of CDs.

The electrochemical oxidation is another top-down method in which bulk carbon source is used as starting material [29]. It’s a simple one-step reaction where stable voltage has been applied between carbon or graphite electrode [30].This method is highly popular among the synthetic methodologies to generate nanoparticles. This method holds various important advantages including reproducible results, scalability, better control over the surface oxidation via changing the potential values and easier means to control operational parameters. The mechanism involves the usage of carbon rich source such as graphite and organic molecules (citric acid, urea) as electrode. The respective electrode has further been dipped in the electrolytic solution of either NaOH or NaCl. Subsequently, the electric voltage is applied to initiate the process of oxidation in carbon material. As a result, the chosen carbon material is transformed in to nanometric range particles. The surface functionalization is also achievable via using this methodology via interaction of formed CDs with solvent or chosen electrolyte. The formation of CDs in electrochemical oxidation is usually achieved via anodic oxidation process [31]. Liu et al. synthesized CDs by using graphite as a working electrode and a mixture of ethanol/NaOH/H2O as the electrolyte system. The obtained particles possessed 4 nm of average size and spherical shape. The quantum yield was around 11.2 % by applying 5 V to the working electrode for 3 h. The superior photoluminescence properties of synthesized CDs have further been utilized in sensing of Fe3 + ions [32]. Li et al. prepared CDs by using carbonized corncobs as starting material via electrochemical oxidation method. The porous corncobs taken as anode and Pt wire taken as counter electrode dipped in 1 M KOH solution. CDs output was 79.8 mgh−1/g with a constant supply of 1 A current for 1 h. The obtained CDs were used as anode in sodium ion batteries with capacity of 263.3 mAh−1 after 1000 cycles at 1Ag−1 [33]. Ma et al. have synthesized fluorescent CDs by using microplasma technique by using isopropanol at atmospheric pressure [34]. The HR-TEM and SAED results showed that prepared CDs displayed narrow size distribution with average size 1.78 nm, spherical shape, amorphous and graphitic nature. The fluorescence study revealed that CDs present excitation dependent emission properties with excitation wavelength range 310–410 nm. Fig. 3c shows that the preparation of fluorescent CDs by using microplasma process by using single precursor isopropanol and their characterization using HR-TEM, SAED and fluorescence spectroscopy. The graphitic solution of K2S2O8 has been employed by the generation of water soluble CDs via using electrochemical method. The existence of large domain density of sp2 type of carbon atom in core of produced CDs has further influenced the optical and luminesce properties of formed particles [35]. The generation of multicolor CDs is also been achievable by using electrochemical method [36]. The variation in the applied voltage has directly influenced the degree of cross linking in formed particles and also influenced the size of developed particles. With the increment in the cross linking in the particles have directly produced particle size broadening and red shift in the luminesce peak of formed particles and enhanced their future scope in bio-imaging application.

The bottom up methodologies includes the conversion of small molecule into CDs by employing hydrothermal, microwave assistant or thermal decomposition techniques [37]. The diverse range of advantages including better control over the size and shape, scalability, versatility has made these methods more usable among researchers for the synthesis of CDs.

The high temperature and pressure conditions are usually employed in hydrothermal method over the precursor source to generate CDs. Diverge range of biomass sources including household kitchen waste or chemical sources such as glucose, citric acid and other carbon containing compounds are initially dissolved in dissolved in solvent medium for the fabrication of CDs [38]. This method is usually considered as one of the most commonly used, simplest, single step, economical and environmental friendly way to generate size controlled CDs (Fig. 4a) [39]. The fabrication mechanism usually consist the heating of precursor source in solvent medium under high pressure and temperature in autoclave. The carbonization of precursor source led to the formation of CDs. The isolation of the formed CDs is usually performed by extracting the solvent using heating the reaction medium above boiling point. The particles formed using this method possessed better solubility, high biocompatibility and quite useful for biomedical and sensing application.

Liu et al. [40] synthesized fluorescent spherical shape CDs from sodium citrate and polyacrylamide by using hydrothermal method. The CDs were average diameter of 2.4 nm and exhibited excitation independent properties. Fig. 4b illustrates the preparation of fluorescent CDs by keeping precursors at 200 °C for 3 h in autoclave. For instance, Chaudhary et al. synthesized CDs by using coconut husk heating at high temperature. The average size of formed CDs was 3.7 nm and possessed spherical shape. The synthesized CDs have higher potential in removal of Victoria blue dye [41] (Fig. 4c). George et al. [42] utilized waste papaya seed for preparation of CDs having spherical shape and average size of 1.45 nm. The developed CDs showed high domains of sp2 carbon core and have possessed higher fluorescent behaviour with emission peak at 405 nm correspond to excitation wavelength 335 nm. The emission properties have further been used for the sensing of Fe3+ ion with detection limit 2.35 µM [42].

Zinc doped CDs have also achieved by using hydrothermal technique. The formed particles displayed higher doping efficiencies and better control over the size and shape of formed particles. Bakier et al. [43] synthesized novel CDs by one step hydrothermal method using expired folic acid tablets as a precursor source. The developed CDs used as photocatalyst under solar irradiation for degradation of organic pollutant sulfamethazine up-to 94 % within 75 min. Zinc oxide doped CDs were successfully prepared by using zinc salt, KOH and sucrose as a starting material. The modified CDs were utilized as photo catalyst for the degradation of organic pollutants including rhodamine B and phenazopyridine [44] (Fig. 4d).

Microwave mediated synthesis have the ability to generate uniform sized CDs with quick reaction time [45]. The microwave radiation has the ability to quickly heat the precursor solution to form homogenous CDs [46]. This method is very fast and produces CDs within minutes. Initially, the carbon containing precursors are irradiated with microwaves wavelength ranging from 1 mm to 1 m to produces heat via ionic conduction in precursor solution. The uniform heating of carbon containing precursor undergoes carbonization. Finally, the particles grow into CDs having size range 1–10 nm with spherical shape. For instance, the dried seeds into 10 ml H3PO4 were employed under microwave conditions for 90 seconds to synthesis CDs. Guanine based CDs were easily prepared via microwave assisted method by Li et al. [47]. The formed particles possessed spherical shape with the average size of 3.75 nm. The developed particles were utilized in selective detection of Ag+ ions with low limit of detection of 90 nM [47]. Zhu and co-workers have used the application of microwave mediated pyrolytic method to generate highly fluorescent, water soluble, cost effective synthesis of CDs within 5 minute of irradiation time [48]. The ease in preparation, solvent free fabrication, fast processing with economical methodology has enhanced the scope of this method in scale up synthesis of CDs ( Fig. 5a) [42].

Nascimento et al. has used the disodium ethylenediammine tetra acetic, sodium thiosulfate and urea as the starting precursor for the synthesis of CDs. The respective effect of the irradiation effect of microwave on the quantum yield has also been observed (Fig. 5b). Just the exposure of 2 min of irradiation has the ability to generate 8.9 % quantum yield in the particles. The fabrication of CDs via this method usually involved the dehydration of the reaction medium followed by polymerization and carbonization. The surface passivation has also been achieved by the different types of functional moieties over the surface of particles. These functional moieties further supported the hydrophilicity and stability in formed CDs [49]. Freshly available cabbage and onions were also used in a household microwave at 700 W for 4 min for the preparation of sphere shaped intense blue fluorescent CDs with average diameter of 12.6 ± 3.16 nm and quantum yield of 15.2 %. The grouping of biomass as starting material and usage of microwave method provided a noticeable greenness to the current procedure (Fig. 5c) [50].

The thermal decomposition approach involves the heating of precursors source at high temperature condition to form CDs [51]. The mechanism based on the heating precursor at high temperature under inert atmosphere. Under high temperature, the chosen precursor source breakdowns into carbon clusters which further condense and produce nano size CDs. The size and physicochemical properties of CDs can be control by adjusting the temperature and precursor source. The functional groups developed over the CDs stabilize them and enhance their stability in various solvent. Chaudhary et al. [52] developed CDs by using bio agar waste, coconut coir, plastic waste and rice has as starting materials using thermal decomposition method [53]. The known amount of the agar waste was subjected to heat at 350°C for 3 h.

The transparent colour of the reaction medium was progressively transformed from colourless to light yellow to brown, indicating the formation of CDs ( Fig. 6). Suraj et al. synthesized CDs by pyrolysis of coffee husk waste at 500 °C. The formed particles possessed spherical shape with average size of 5.38 nm [54]. The fabricated CDs applied to enhance the solar photovoltaic applications [54]. Li at el. developed nitrogen doped CDs by using citric acid and ethylene diamine as a starting material [55]. The formed CDs have possessed superior photoluminescence properties and useful in sensing of Fe3+ ions [55]. However, on analysing the available literature regarding the fabrication of CDs using different methods, it has been observed that the use of different types of bio waste as starting precursor source have the ability to produce biocompatible CDs. The method offers high economical processing and saleable methodology for producing CDs. The usage of waste precursor source offers dual advancement of overcoming solid waste management and production of biocompatible fluorophore for environmental remediation applications. The optical properties of the formed CDs have directly been influenced by the chosen precursor source. The methodology provided sustainability and cost-effectiveness in preparing CDs. The usage of milder reaction conditions has further supported the positive impact of this methodology under industrial sector. However, the reproducibility and standardization of formed CDs is the main challenging factors during the synthesis of CDs by using bio waste resources. Additionally, the purification of formed CDs obtained using biowaste is highly complexed and multi-step.

Ultrasonic assistant approach is another well-known method for the development of CDs [56]. This method has the ability to generate non-toxic, fast, high yield CDs by using low temperature and less costly instrumentation [57].The mechanism involved the usage of high frequency ultrasonic waves over carbon containing precursor. The fluctuations produces due to ultrasonic waves caused formation and collapse of bubbles inside the solution known as cavitation effect. Due to this effect temperature and pressure of solution increased, which further caused the breakdown of molecular bonds in bigger size particles and lead to the formation of CDs.

For instance, Mallakpour et al. synthesized poly (ethylene terephthalate) derived CDs using ultrasonic waves and utilized the fabricated CDs for eradiation of methylene blue dye from wastewater [60]. Wang et al. fabricated high fluorescent CDs from maltose using ultrasonic wave assistant reaction. The formed particles were modified with ZnIn2S4 to enhance their photocatalytic activities [61].

Thus, the choice of synthetic method should be based on the overall impact on the yield, photostability, biocompatibility, shelf-life, physicochemical characteristic of formed particles. Thus, constant aim should be inculcated available methods to refine synthetic techniques to better understand the intrinsic properties and their applications. Table 1 summarized the various synthetic approaches used for the fabrication of CDs for different applications.

It is mentioned that there are no universal method for separation and purifications of CDs. Some methods were developed such as dialysis, centrifugation, electrophoresis, filtration and chromatography based upon nature of product formed after synthesis. Dialysis is a method used for to separate low molecular weight residual precursors from larger size CDs using semipermeable membrane [111]. This method is ideal for removing low molecular mass impurities such as salts and unreacted precursor. This is simple, inexpensive method but some disadvantages also associated with it this method takes several days depending upon size of impurities and unable to remove impurities having similar size CDs. Some other method like centrifugation, filtration also combined with dialysis to achieve maximum purification of CDs.

Electrophoresis is a purifying technique used in a conductive medium in which particle separation occurs position when an electric field is introduced [112]. The movement of these particles are controlled by their size, shape, and charge in addition to the current temperature. This method is laborious and can be used when the particles cannot be effectively separated by dialysis or column chromatography. Additionally, it makes it possible to physically separate positive and negative charged particles. Water-soluble substances separated by electrophoresis. This method effectively separate CDs from impurities depends upon size and charge. Under electric field mixture containing CDs, by product unreacted precursor migrate with different depends upon their charge, functional group and size. This method excellent for removal of charged species. Ineffectiveness for uncharged impurities, limited scalability and requirement of specialized equipment restrict its usability.

Another method for sorting and purifying CDs according to variations in size, shape, and density is centrifugation. Bigger or denser particles are separated from smaller or less dense ones by applying strong centrifugal forces to a CDs mixture [113]. This method is essential for producing homogeneous CDs with reliable characteristics. This method provides precise control over size distribution of CDs, effective for large sample purification and helpful in retaining optical and structural integrity of CDs. This method take time for multiple time centrifugation to achieve high purity of CDs makes it little bit time consumable. Further high speed centrifuge require for high purity sample not available in all labs make its limited applicability.

Chromatography is another well-known method for separation of components from mixture based upon their chemical and physical properties such as size, charge, interaction with stationary phase [114]. This method effectively removed impurities and ensure consistent quality of CDs. This method applicable for both small as well large scale CDs purification. This method also have many disadvantages including high cost of column, skill technical expertise requirement. Filtration is extensively used method of purification of CDs [115]. This method is cost effective and significant in removal of unreacted precursor and larger size impurities with appropriate pore size of filter paper. The blocking of pores of filter at high concentration, loss of material and lack of fine purifications are the major drawback of this method. Thus purifications of CDs not depends only on single purifications method but multiple methods required to attained maximal purifications also each methods have their own pros and cons.

Purification of CDs put significant impact over its catalytic applications. The durability, efficiency, and selectivity of CDs in catalytic applications are all strongly impacted by their purity. Impure CDs contain blocked active sites which decreased their catalytic efficiency. Presence of impurity also cause side reactions and aggregations of CDs which further enhance the hindrance of catalytic performance of CDs. The CDs well-defined surface functional groups, regulated sizes, and ideal electrical characteristics are ensured by adequate purification and are essential for different catalytic applications. Further, purification showed significant impact on fundamental research. Impurities cause inconsistent in experimental results, which makes research and applications less reproducible. The fluorescent CDs quenched by impurities which lower the quantum yield and effect the outcomes of research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of quantum yield on catalytic applications
ce:section.ce:para: Photon emission efficiency of CDs are measure by calculating its quantum yield (QY). It is the ratio of number of photon emitted to number of photon absorbed. QY is significant because it relates with the photoluminescence properties of CDs important in various applications including sensing, bio imaging and catalysis. There are many factors effecting the QY of CDs including doping, surface modifications, synthesis conditions, inner defects and type of precursor chosen. QY play significant role in deciding the catalytic properties of CDs. In CDs based photocatalyst having high QY enhanced the catalytic activity by intensifying the light absorption and emission capacity, promoting charge carriers generation and improving energy transfer rate. High QY also helped in strengthen the electrochemical performance of CDs by boosting charge carriers mobilities, increasing interaction capacity. Moreover, QY of CDs can be increased by surface engineering the surface, doping, and by adjusting the band gap. In short, boosting CDs QY improves their catalytic activity by making light absorption, charge separation, and energy transfer more efficient. They are therefore very promising for a range of energy-efficient and sustainable catalytic applications.

A combination of various characterizing technique are used for comprehensive studies of surface morphology, structure, chemical composition, optical behaviour of CDs [116]. The notable size of CDs is less than 10 nm with spherical shape. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Field emission microscopy (SEM) and Atomic force microscopy (AFM) explained the shape, size, distribution, topology of CDs. Whereas X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectroscopy analysed crystalline/amorphous nature, presence of defect and degree of disorder within the CDs. The Fourier transformation infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) determined the surface functional group and elemental composition and chemical state of compound. The optical properties of CDs are explored by UV–visible spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy and time resolved photoluminescence. The amount of charge present over CDs is determined by Zeta-potential studies. The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) reveals the elemental composition of the CDs. Moreover, the surface area and porosity of CDs which is important for many applications such as drug delivery, adsorption are examined by Brunauer- Emmett- Teller (BET) analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical and electronic structure of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The 0 dimensional spherical or quasi spherical particles are usually denoted as the CDs. Further they have possessed the crystalline or amorphous nature. Broadly, CDs are divided into GQDs, CNDs or CQDs depending on the nature of their chemical structure. The major component of GQDs is π-conjugated sp2 hybridized graphene sheets. They are highly crystalline in nature. However, CQDs have also possessed the lesser degree of crystallinity and more defects in comparison to GQDs. The reported literature reviled the existence of oxygen containing functional groups over the surface of CDs. CDs usually acquired graphite/graphite oxide like structure. Through thermal calcination, weak lattice spacing were observed in CQDs with agarose waste as precursor source [117]. The hydrothermal treatment of pearl millet has the ability to produce luminescent CDs with –COOH and OH containing functional groups which further increased the ability of formed particles to complex with different range of analytes and enhanced their environmental remediation application ( Fig. 7a) [118]. The amorphous nature was verified by the presence of small and sharp pointed peaks in XRD spectra (Fig. 7b).

The electronic structure of CDs has been explained by the well-known molecular orbital theories [119]. The computational means have also been used to understand the electronic structure of CDs [120]. CDs prepared via diverse methods have usually possessed sp2 hybridized core and have displayed n→π* and π→π* [121]. The increment in the aromatic rings in the core structure has directly influenced the energy gap between the π-transition states. The natures of the existing functional groups have also influence the n→π* transitions CDs. The sensitivity of the starting material has a direct influence on the band gap value and defect states in CDs. Usually, CDs possessed defect-bound excitons, defect-charged excitons and electron– hole recombination effect. The graphite core in CDs holds disordered (D-band) and sp2 (G-band) carbon. The changes in the pH of the reaction medium have displayed the direct influence over the surface functional groups on CDs. The corresponding changes further affect the delocalization of π electron cloud, and influenced the dynamic equilibrium between the defect-bound excitons and defect-charged excitons (Fig. 7c) [122].

The microstructural parameters and activity relation has also been established by the help of reduced molecular models or DFT or first principle calculations [123]. The theoretical calculations further supported with constructional strategies and reduced logic system to get insight the unique optical and luminescence performance of CDs (Fig. 7d).

DFT calculations with 6 different types of models including chemical modifications at ωB97X-D/def2- SV(P) has displayed the shift in absorption and luminesce peak with reference to the increment in particles size. The molecular dynamic studies have supported the structural defects in CDs were mainly associated with the solvation of sp2 network in CDs. The existence of surface defects via oxidation or surface passivation has further analysed through computational studies. From the data, it has been observed that the surface defects have the ability to capture exciton centres. However, the multicolour emission intensities in CDs are due to the non-perfect sp2 domains in the core of CDs particles. The quantum confinement effect, conductivity, band gap tunability, charges carrier dynamic makes CDs as a prominent electro catalyst [124].

The quantum confinement effect occurred in nanoparticles, as size of particle decreased [125]. There is significant change in electronic properties of CDs due to the decrement in the size [126,127]. CDs show quantum confinement effect due to its nano range size and presence of conjugated pi electron system. The various types of properties of CDs are influence due to quantum confinement effect. As the size of CDs decreased, the resultant band gap becomes broaden. This result in blue shifting of photoluminescence properties in CDs. Due to the presence of discrete band gap, CDs are highly useful in sensing and optoelectronics as it facilitates charge separation and transport. The combination between quantum confinement and surface functional groups provides tenable emission capabilities to CDs. Further, the ability to absorb light, size dependent fluorescence and tenable optical properties makes CDs as useful material in photocatalysis, bio imaging and in energy storage devices. The band gap modification via varying the shape, size and surface chemistry of CDs helped in modifying the photoluminescence abilities in CDs and enhanced their potential scope in sensing [128,129]. The conductivity is other important properties possessed by CDs which further affects the catalytic properties of CDs. Generally, CDs are semi-conducting in nature and there conductivities can be intensified by doping with heteroatoms and other conductive metals species [130]. The catalytic efficiency of CDs further intensified by favourable charge carrier dynamic behaviour including quick charge transfer and separation [131,132].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and luminescence properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The promising optical properties of CDs make them a prominent catalyst in environment remediation, organic synthesis, and oxygen and hydrogen generation activities [133]. CDs usually absorb range of 230–350 nm. However, the surface modification has the ability to shift the absorption capacity of CD to longer wavelength. The respective π-π* and n-π* transitions are mainly responsible for the shoulder peaks in CDs. The nature of the existing functional group as well as the solvent system has also influenced the absorption spectra in CDs ( Fig. 8). The wide light absorption, tunable band gap, strong photoluminescence, large stroke shift, synergistic effect, and photo stability in CDs mainly support the photo catalytic activities and their potential prospects in environmental remediation applications [134,135]. CDs show strong photoluminescence properties in UV–visible and near infrared region and tunable emission wavelength [136]. Moreover, CDs exhibits high photoluminescence quantum yield indicate efficient light emission [137]. The excitation-dependent emission ability in CDs supports their potential in bioimaging applications. The photo induced electron transfer ability in CDs supports their catalytic activity in degrading harmful pollutants via transferring electron from donor to acceptor molecules via non-radiative pathways. The quantum yield of CDs can be modified by suitable surface modification [138]. CDs exhibits large stroke shift which indicates the large difference between the peak of adsorption and emission wavelengths [139]. Further, large strokes values prevent the reabsorption of emitted light and hence enhance the photoluminescence efficiency of light [140].

The photocatalytic efficiency of CDs further enhance through synergistic effect by combining CDs with other photocatalyst (TiO2, ZnO, Fe2O3) [141–143]. CDs showed excellent photo stability and can be used for long time under exposure of light.

CDs shows other significant properties such as reactive oxygen species production (ROS), photoredox reactions, plasmonic effect, pH control behaviour, charge separation, charge transfer and redox activity [145]. CDs shows plasmonic effect and have the ability to enhance local electromagnetic field and absorb light at particular wavelength [146]. It increased the effective energy transfer and light absorption tendency to reactant [147]. As a result, CDs have the ability to accelerate the photocatalytic reactions [148]. Li et al. formed Au/CDs/Cds hybrid with excellent plasmonic effect and synthesized composites demonstrate superior photocatalytic efficiency for hydrogen generation [149]. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation is another property shown by CDs [150]. Under exposure of light CDs produces highly reactive ROS such as singlet oxygen (O2), superoxide radical (O2 *-) and hydroxyl radical (OH*) which are very reactive can participates in various oxidation reaction, makes CDs an efficient catalyst ( Fig. 9) [151]. Ali et al. investigate the role of ROS under visible light irradiation in P25-CDs photocatalyst for photodegradation of rhodamine B dye [152]. Furthermore, Chen et al. demonstrate the significant role of ROS generated during photocatalytic activity of TiO2 doped CDs in water purification process [153]. CDs show photo-redox reactions in which electrons are transferred under the exposures of light. This property enable CDs to act as an excellent catalyst in various organic conversions including reduction, oxidation and coupling reactions [154]. Qian et al. explored the photo-redox capability of CDs based catalyst in biological processes [155]. CDs exhibits redox activity act as both electron acceptors and donors which is crucial for catalytic activities involving exchange of electrons. In spite of electric, optical and electric properties, CDs shows some other properties. such as water solubility, biocompatibility, large surface area and thermal and chemical stability [156]. The solubility of CDs is determined by a multitude of factors, including the precursor and the solvent. Thus, the CDs can be hydrophilic or hydrophobic. Due to hydrophilic nature of CDs, they easily dispersed in aqueous media, prevent aggregation and ensuring high catalytic activity [157]. CDs possesses small size, low toxicity makes it biocompatible thus can be used in biomedicine applications. These properties helped them to maintain catalytic efficiency in various applications such as water purifications, environment monitoring, aqueous and biological system [158]. CDs derived from bio mass Biocompatibility of CDs refers the interaction of CDs with biological system without imparting any harmful impact on it [159]. This character makes CDs interactive with biological molecules such as protein, nucleic acid and enzymes without denaturing them. CDs have large surface area due to their small size. This large surface area of CDs offers several advantages including enhance drug delivery, catalytic activity, photoluminescence and better up the sensing capabilities [160]. Thermal stability of CDs depends upon the presence of degree of graphitization and hydrogen bonding and chemical stability of CDs determine by presence of functional groups which makes CDs less likely react with other reactive species. Thermal and chemical stability allow the CDs many benefits such as enhance compatibility and durability, constant fluorescence and cost effectiveness [161]. The stability of CDs maintains the catalytic properties over a long span even under harsh condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Doping and surface functionalization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs generated via the conventional means have possessed low value of photoluminescence quantum yield. The presence of diverse range of functional groups including hydroxyl, carboxyl or amine or epoxy groups has the tendency to restrict the emission intensity in prepared CDs. Additionally, the shorter wavelength displayed by the blue emitting CDs have lesser ability to penetrate biological samples and restricted their bioimaging application. Thus the photoluminescence and fluorescence abilities of CDs prepared by conventional methods need to be modified for their wider potential in tissues screening in biological samples. To achieve the objective, two widely employed methods include: the doping of CDs with different types of heteroatoms or modifying the properties of CDs with surface functionalization. The subsequent section deals with the brief description about the effect of doping and surface modification on CDs.

The dispersion ability, solubility, optical and luminescence properties of CDs can be easily altered by doping the carbon dots with diverse range of metallic or non-metallic atomic ions [162]. The doped CDs have possessed advanced properties which further enhanced their potential in sensing, biomedical applications, catalysis, photo degradation, toxin sensing in food products and many more [163]. The incorporation of dopant during the synthesis of CDs has the ability to modify the surface state by existing their presence on the exterior surface of CDs, which in turn amend the fluorescence emission behavior and quantum yield of CDs. The single step processing has further supported the addition of doping during the synthesis of CDs via conventional methods. Additionally, the incorporation of dopant in the core of formed CDs have the ability to modulate the quantum confinement effect in CDs by generating extra energy levels in the energy bands of CDs. Thus, the optical and fluorescence abilities of doped CDs are better in comparison to bare CDs. The synergistic impact of dopant on the absorption properties of CDs has further modulated the band gap valued of CDs and displayed better control over the opto-luminesce properties of CDs [164]. The incorporation of particular metal ion as dopant has further enhanced their selectivity and sensitivity towards toxin sensing. Various types of metallic dopant such as Cu, Fe, Zn, Mn, La, Mo, Co have been employed so for enhancing the optical properties of CDs [165].

However, the larger size of metal ion hindered the uniform doping of added dopant in CDs. Furthermore, the toxic nature of metal ion has further restricted the application doped CDs in biomedical field [166]. To overcome this restriction, the addition of non-metallic dopant in CDs is quite common to modulate the physicochemical properties of prepared carbon dots. The comparable size of non-metals with CDs displayed uniform doping of non-metallic ions during the fabrication of CDs. Upto now, different types of non-metallic dopants including N, S, P, Se, B, F, Cl, Br or I have been used so far for the modification in the optical properties of CDs [167]. However, the N/S doping is quite commonly used by researchers for the amendments in the luminescence properties of CDs ( Fig. 10).

Diverse types of starting precursors such as hair, caffeine, urea, waste garlic peels, L-cysteine, m-phenylenediamine, sulfuric acid, biological waste, rose petals, green algae blooms, Cannabis, Aloe vera leaf, green tea residues have been widely used to produce co-doped CDs ( Table 2) For instance, Niu et al. synthesized nitrogen doped CDs as photocatalyst for organic synthesis by using citric acid and ethylenediammine as a precursor [168]. The impact of nitrogen concentration on catalytic efficiency has also been verified. The low concentration of nitrogen showed higher catalytic efficiency and it decreases with increase in nitrogen concentration. Wang et al. enhanced the efficiency of visible light CDs/g-C3N4 based photocatalyst doping with nitrogen for degradation of indomethacin. The introduction of nitrogen increased the charge separation, narrowing the band gap [169]. Wu et al. synthesized CDs based photocatalyst for photo oxidation reactions [170]. They improved poor electron transfer and low conductivity of catalyst by converting CDs into Cu-N-doped CDs [170]. The doped CDs influenced the electron accepting and donating capacities to 2.5 and 1.5 times as compared to its bare counterpart [170]. Jamila et al. prepared N doped CDs with CuO nano leaves. The modification showed the slight [171] increased in band gap of photocatalyst and applied further to degrade methylene orange dye [171]. The composite showed better degradation efficiency than pure CuO catalyst [171]. The nitrogen doping enhance the photoluminescence intensity, chemical stability and quantum yield whereas sulphur doped CDs shows better catalytic efficiency [172].

Phosphorous doping increases the electron density and boost electrical conductivity[177]. Additionally, the stability of CDs has been easily modulated by the addition of sulphur doping [178]. The emission trap state in CDs is easily affected by the addition of S doping, which in turn modulate the photoluminescence properties of CDs. However, the addition of halogen ion as dopant has the ability to decrease the band gap values among the HOMO and LUMO energy levels via introducing the extra energy level between the two. The phosphorescence properties of CDs have also been influenced via halogen ion as dopants.

The catalytic performance of halogen dopant CDs are more advanced as compare to bare CDs [179]. CDs with long wavelength emission near infrared region are easily achievable via using the halogen doping in CDs. The higher electronegative nature of halogen dopant has the ability amend the π electronic structure in CDs. Additionally, the conjugated domains of sp2 has been significantly modified by the incorporation of halogen as dopant ion in CDs [180]. The halogen dopant has the ability to tune the emission wavelengths in CDs and displayed multicolour emission and enhanced their potential in optoelectronic devices [181]. The catalytic activity of CDs has further been modified by halogen doping through electron-hole separation and movement of charge carriers [182]. Thus offers, an economical and sustainable alternative in energy transference reactions.

Surface modifications in CDs mainly altered the exterior surface to change their properties through chemical or physical processes without affecting the core structure in CDs. The surface modifications includes attaching functional groups such as –COOH, -NH2, and –OH etc. over the surface of CDs. The surface functionalization includes the chemical functional groups, covalent and non-covalent bond like electrostatic force of attraction, hydrogen bonding and van der waals forces of attractions between organic or inorganic molecules and CDs. The surface modifications further improved the stability, dispersibility and biocompatibility of CDs. The surface modifications also direct the CDs particles towards target drug deliveries and biosensing applications [193]. Moreover, surface modifications prevent the agglomeration of formed particles. The surface modification of CDs with organic molecules, and composite with metal oxide (CuO, TiO2) has the ability to amend the catalytic efficiency of CDs [194]. DFT calculations suggest that the surface modifications in CDs have direct influence over the conductivity values which in turn supports the catalytic efficiency in formed particles. The surface area and number of available active sites are also influenced by the surface functionalization of CDs. The antibacterial potential of CDs has also been modified by the proper surface functionalization. For instance, the surface modification of CDs with lauryl betaine has displayed both antibacterial and bacterial distinction abilities [195]. The formed particles were able to selective label the gram positive bacterial strain in reference to gram negative bacteria. In an another report, Emeterio et al. modified glass carbon electrode (GCE) surface by N,S-doped CDs for increasing the electrochemical detection activity of probe for sensing of organic species such as ascorbic acid, salicylic acid and cysteine [196]. Pawar et al. have prepared the bipyridine-modified chitosan based carbon dots (CDs) to sense trace amount of water by fluorescence sensing technique [197]. The surface modification has a direct impact on the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process and support the detection of water molecules in organic solvents ( Fig. 11). Wang et al. [198] developed the phosphorous-doped carbon dots implanted intra layered carbon nitride nano sheets (PCD-C3N4) photocatalyst with phosphorous-nitrogen (P-N) bridging. Implanting P to CDs enlarged the π-π conjugated electronic structure and P-N bridge increased the delocalization effect and facilitated the intermolecular charge transfer in CDs. The modified PCD-C3N4 exhibited the efficient photo degradation of bisphenol and tetracycline than bare CDs, raising 76 % and 100 % respectively [198]. Catechol-grafted PEG has also been used for the modification of CDs [199]. The formed particles have displayed cytotoxic impact in MTT assay. Pei et al. intensified the conductivity and surface area of carbon nanotubes based electro catalyst by introducing B,N functionalized CDs [200]. The modified electro catalyst exhibited the fast selectron transfer rate and excellent performance towards oxygen reduction reactions [200]. Bourlinos et al. prepared Fe (III) – functionalized CDs an efficient photoluminescence redox catalyst for decomposition of hydrogen peroxide and hydrogenation of olefins [201]. The iron ions attached to surface endow the CDs with excellent conversion and selectivity (100 %) and superior catalytic activities [201].

Thus, it’s not wrong to say the doping and surface modification has direct influence over the structural and optical properties of CDs and further supports their application in photocatalysis, bio-sensing, bio-imaging, agricultural sector, solar cell devices and many more. Additionally, the high biocompatibility of CDs in comparison to commercial dyes with amendable fluorescence properties with superior photostability and resistance to photobleaching has supported their application in photocatalysis and drug delivery applications. The subsequent section highlights the potential application of CDs in catalysis with specific emphasis on the photo-catalytic degradation of harmful toxins.

CDs have displayed the diverse range of applications and possessed significant potential role in many industrial sectors have found various applications across many industries. CDs are also useful in light emitting diodes, phototherapy, PDT applications, gene and drug delivery applications [202]. The amazing photo-stable nature, ease in preparation and controllable optical properties have supported their potential role in sensing of harmful toxins ( Fig. 12a) [203]. The ease in surface modification has amplified their photo-induced electron transfer ability and helpful in enhancing their carrier abilities [204]. The charge generation, conductivity and electron hole separation properties further helped CDs as catalysts in organic transformation reactions and energy storage devices (Figs. 12b and 12c) [205,206]. A variety of organic reactions such as carbon-carbon coupling, oxidation, and reduction are catalysed by CDs [207]. For instance, Sarma et al. synthesized visible light induced increment in the multi- catalytic activities of CDs for aerobic carbon-carbon bond formation [208]. They investigate the use of hydrogen sulfate-decorated CDs as a photocatalyst for the dehydrogenative cross-coupling of xanthenes with ketones, arenes, and 1,3-dicarbonyl compounds, which demonstrated excellent selectivity and efficiency when exposed to visible light [208]. Majumdar et al. synthesized magnetic iron oxide–CDs composites and further utilized as effective catalyst for cyclooxidative tandem synthesis of quinazolelinones in aqueous medium using alcohol as a starting material [209]. Hasani et al. developed CDs functionalized with L-lysine for the C-N bond formation in choline chloride urea [210].

Further, they demonstrated that Lys-CDs were a powerful catalyst in conversion of aniline into phenyl urea [210]. Ren at al. formed composite of copper nanoparticles on CDs for boosting chemo selective reduction [211]. The prepared nano catalyst achieved over 99 % selectivity for conversion of 4-nitrostyrene into 4-aminostyrene in aqueous solvent under the irradiation of visible light [211]. It is a major challenge to develop a highly efficient and highly selective catalyst for oxidation of alkanes under mild conditions. The commercially used catalysts were suffered from low efficiency, huge waste production, and low selectivity. To overcome this problem, Liu et al. synthesized composites of gold nanoparticle with carbon dot (Au/CDs) as an effective photo catalyst with 63.8 % conversion efficiency and 99.9 % selectivity towards oxidation of cyclohexane into cyclohexanone in presence of H2O2 under visible light at room temperature [212]. Onat et al. [213] synthesized caffeine based CDs supported cobalt catalysts for generation of hydrogen via hydrolysis of potassium borohydride.

Dehkordi et al. successfully design recyclable amphiphilic homogeneous catalyst of CDs [214]. They further functionalized the catalyst with introducing 1-aminopropyl-3-methyl-imidazolium chloride and stearic acid. The developed catalyst significantly promoted the oxidative cracking of variety of olefins into corresponding aldehyde [214]. The catalyst has the ability to transform 87 % substrates into related aldehyde in presence of hydrogen peroxide and pure water [214]. Sahu et al. used gold doped water soluble photocatalyst in visible light photo conversion of carbon dioxide (CO2) into acetic acid under irradiation of UV–visible light [215]. The photo conversion efficiency has further been enhanced by increasing CO2 pressure over aqueous solution of photocatalyst [215]. Tang et al. used CDs for the development of hybrid with nickel iron layer double hydroxide [216]. The prepared nano-hybrid utilized as efficient electro catalyst in oxygen evolution reactions. The hybrid showed high electro catalytic activity with over potential of 235 mV in 1 M KOH at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and highly stable for oxygen generation [216]. Ji et al. [217] prepared CDs based catalyst decorated over palladium(Pd) and utilized it in conversion of vanillin to 2-methoxy-4-methylphenol. Pd doped CDs were prepared by glucose and urea via microwave assisted method for 10 min and vanillin hydrodeoxygenation was studied in water and methanol using developed catalyst ( Fig. 13a).

The superior electrical conductivity, high surface area, stability makes CDs as a suitable candidate for energy conversion and energy storage applications [218]. In energy storage applications, CDs are used in supercapicitors and batteries [219]. CDs increase the rate of charge/discharge and electric conductivity by increasing the surface area of electrode. Moreover, CDs can be utilized in electrode formation in batteries such as lithium ion, sodium ion and lithium sulphur [220]. In energy conversion activities, CDs has further been used in solar panel, fuel cells and as a photo catalyst for energy transformation [221]. In solar plates, CDs layer showed enhancement in light absorption and electron transport [221]. As an electro catalyst, CDs catalyse the oxygen reduction and hydrogen production method, result into the increment in the efficiency and stability of fuel cells [222]. Wang et al. constructed CDs based composites films for simultaneously harvesting raindrop energy and boosting solar energy transformation efficiency in hybrid cells [223]. The noticeable increase in transmittance in the visible spectral region at an ideal loading of carbon dots in the composite raises the solar cells short-circuit current density, which further raises the power conversion efficiency from 13.6 % to 14.6 % [223]. Wei et al. studied the role of CDs in production of energy storage electrode [224]. The addition of small quantity of CDs in traditional electrode system exhibited improvements in cycling life, enlarging capacity and columbic efficiency12. Yushan et al. [225] manufactured wood-based composite phase change materials (WPCMs), fabricated with CDs. The luminous thermochromic properties were developed for the purpose of visual converting and storing solar-thermal energy. The composite exhibited high latent heat of fusion, effective stability over 150 cycles, excellent shape stability and superior thermal stability below 210 °C [225]. Fig. 13b shows the schematic diagram for fluorescent thermochromic wood-based composite phase change materials (WPCMs) construction and its thermochromic mechanism. Yu et al. synthesized the 3-D lignin based activated carbon composites as an electrode material for high performance supercapicitors (SCs) [226]. The hydrophilicity and ultrafine size of CDs increased the affinity of electrode towards aqueous solutions which result into enhancement in surface area and decreased ionic diffusion path [226]. After addition of CDs, significant improvement in SCs properties observed such as capacitance improved from 125 to 308 Fg−1, diffusion coefficient increased by 25 times than pristine activated carbon [226]. Zhang et al. synthesized 3D nitrogen doped porous carbon framework were utilized in lithium-ion storage batteries (LIBs) [227]. The highly nitrogen doped CDs based LIBs displayed high charged/discharged capacities, significant rate and cycling performance [227]. Pal et al. assembled 3D crystalline structures via complexation between Zn ions and CDs [228]. The capacity of the electrodes to store charge and perform better electrochemically was aided by the crystallinity of used CDs [228]. Ultimately, the produced crystalline assembly was effectively used to create carbon dot-based supercapicitors electrodes that exhibited a high energy density (20 Whkg−1 at 110 W kg−1) and an exceptional specific capacitance (743.2 F/g) [228]. Dongna et al. synthesized Far-Red CDs as an efficient light-harvesting agent for improving conversion efficiency from solar energy to light energy. The prepared CDs are an effective converter, converting ultraviolet A (UV-A) light to far-red emission between 625 and 800 nm, which directly absorb and utilize by chloroplast [229]. Xinghui et al. [230] designed carbonized wood coated CDs as phase change materials with thermal energy conversion capacity. Fig. 13c represents the preparation of the CW-CDs-PEG, and the process of light-to-heat conversion and storage. Yang et al. synthesized CDs-carbon matrix based upon mix bio gels via calcination-activation method [231]. The developed matrix exhibited the excellent potential in electric double layer capacitor [231]. However, to limit the scope of this review, we are hereby restricting us to discuss the catalytic properties of CDs in environment remediation applications including photo-degradation of harmful toxins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo catalytic degradation of pollutants using CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The rapid industrialization and pollution growth has escalated the release of harmful toxins in environment [232]. The deteriorating influence of pollutants possessed serious threat for survival of living flora and fauna on earth [233]. Up to now diverse methods have been employed to treat these harmful toxins, however, the advanced photo-catalytic properties of CDs played significant contribution towards the elimination of harmful pollutants and demonstrated great potential toward environment remediation applications [234].

CDs as a photo catalyst exhibits significant efficiency for degrading dyes, pesticides, drugs and other organic toxins present in various water bodies [235]. Beker et al. developed iron and nitrogen containing CDs for wastewater treatment [236]. The catalyst degrades methylene blue dye nearly 100 % within 60 min [236]. Pirsaheb et al. [237] demonstrate the reactivity of newly synthesized bio-polymer based CDs an efficient catalyst for green oxidation of phenol. The catalyst activate the decomposition of H2O2 by generating free hydroxide radicals and degrading 99 % phenol in 20 min. in presence of 12 mM H2O2 [237].

The usage of solar energy resources for transforming harmful toxins seems to be the promising way to tackle water pollution. Thus, the development of advanced photocatalysis is an efficient way to degrade diverse range of lethal toxins released from industrial sector. In this regard, the usage of economical, highly stable, with superior photo-catalytic activity in CDs supports their role in photo-degradation ( Fig. 14a). For instance, Saini et al. synthesized sunlight active N,S-codoped CDs as photocatalyst for the removal of toxic hexavalent Cr (VI) ions form the aqueous source [238]. Bhati et al. demonstrate sunlight-induced photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue dye by using Mg-N-embedded CDs [239]. The rate of photodegradation was 6 time faster under the influence of sunlight in comparison with artificial visible light [239]. Selim et al. investigated the synergic effects CDs and Pd nanoparticle composites for the enhancement in the sonocatalytic performance for rhodamine B degradation in dark condition [240]. The modified sonocatalyst achieved the 99 % degradation of dye in 5 minutes. Moreover, the synergic effect of CDs and Pd nanoparticles helped to increase the catalytic efficiency of nano composites [240]. Mukherjee et al. used sunlight driven CDs embedded ZnO nanocomposite (NC) as an efficient catalyst for degrading 98 % of ciprofloxacin drug in110 min [241]. The rate of photo degradation efficiency of NC was 3 times more than pristine ZnO [241]. A new visible light driven effective composite photocatalyst of BaZrO3/CDs was produced by Patra et al. for hydrogen generation and methylene blue dye degradation [242]. The composite showed highest hydrogen production efficiency (670 µmol/h/g) with quantum yield of 4 % and maximum methylene blue degradation efficiency of 90 % [242].

Cui et al. developed N, S-anchored single atom catalyst derived from domestic waste for 90–100 % degradation of organic pollutant [245]. The fluorescent Phyllanthus emblica derived N-doped CDs were synthesized by Arul et al. for investigating their catalytic potential in detoxification of textile effluents [246]. Huang et al. developed environment friendly CDs/ZnFe2O4 photocatalyst for removal of NO (one of the secondary aerosol precursors) derived from combustion of fossil fuel [247]. The photo-catalytic degradation of MB dye is further enhanced by using the chlorine doped CDs particles [248]. The doping effect of Cl over CDs have significantly enhanced the absorption rate of MB dye and possessed the ability to degrade MB molecules via irradiating the sample with visible light source. The amendments in the electron hole separation in Cl doped CDs further enhanced the degradation efficiency in comparison to bare CDs. The up-conversion luminescence property of CDs were also utilized by Xu et al. to prepare the composites of CDs with ZnO nanoparticles by using economical and facile synthetic route [243]. In comparison to bare CDs, or ZnO, nanomaterials, the developed composite have possessed higher light absorption ability with visible light driven degradation ability towards rhodamine B dye (Fig. 14b). The presence of CDs with ZnO has directly influenced the photo-generated electrons and holes in ZnO and produced the red shift in the absorption band of chosen semiconducting material. Under the exposure of visible light, carbon atoms have the tendency to come in to crystalline lattice of ZnO from CDs. This movement has created the new energy levels in the energy bands of ZnO. Thus reduction in band gap value was observed which further increase the light absorption ability in the composites. The electron transference from CDs to the conduction band of ZnO further supported the photo-degradation of dye molecules. The separation of charges in the composite system is further supported by CDs molecules through trapping the electrons from ZnO particles. The up-conversion ability in CDs further helped to utilize longer wavelength of light to produce more electron during the photo degradation process. Similarly, the potential of N doped ZnO/CDs were explored for the photo degradation of MB dye under UV-light exposure [244]. The introduction of heter-atom has provided the electron trap or electron acceptor behaviour in CDs particles (Fig. 14c). However, the band gap value of N doped ZnO/CDs is significantly low i.e. 2.81 eV in comparison to 3.53 eV for ZnO and 2.95 eV for N-ZnO particles.

The superior electron donating and accepting abilities in photo induced CDs lead to the separation of electron –hole and supported the CDs molecules to act as effective electron moderator [249]. Along with CDs have the ability to display spectral transformer aptitude in reaction medium. Additionally, the existence of adsorption center and photosensitizing abilities of CDs supported their photo catalytic efficiency during the degradation of harmful toxins. Moreover, light absorption, charge separation and migration, oxidation and reduction reaction are also responsible for the catalytic action in CDs. There are following steps in photocatalytic mechanism of CDs ( Fig. 15):

There are various factors influencing the photocatalytic performance of CDs including doping, surface functional groups, band gap and integration of other catalyst. For instance, the incorporation of heteroatoms in CDs alters the electronic properties. The doping enhances the charge separation efficiency in CDs and extends the light absorption capability of CDs in higher wavelength range. Band gap optimization plays an important role in determining the photocatalytic efficiency of CDs. It decides the range of light absorb by CDs. By adjusting the band gap for a particular wavelength of light source, we are able to modulate the photocatalytic efficiency of CDs. The size of CDs is prominent factor in adjusting the bandgap of CDs. The small sized CDs possessed the wider the band gap values due to quantum confinement effect. However, the larger sized CDs possess narrower band gap, favours low energy light absorption. The band gap modification has the ability to increase the photocatalytic efficiency of CDs in sunlight which makes it more economical favourable. Further, band gap adjustment reduces the recombination rate of charge carrier by supplying enough energy to electron and holes for migration from surface and improves the availability of charge carrier for redox reactions. Thus careful engineering of bandgap values in CDs has opened up wider range of applications of CDs in CO2 conversion, water splitting, and pollutant degradation [250]. The composites of CDs with other materials enhance the catalytic activity of CDs by improving charge separation, reactant interaction, broadening light absorption spectrum and synergistic effect. The synergistic effect improves the photocatalytic efficiency of CDs. When materials having different intrinsic properties interact with CDs, it has the ability to work together in complementary manner and supported the catalytic efficiency of CDs. For instance, the composite of CDs with ZnO exhibited notable enhancement in photocatalytic performance due to improved charge separation efficiency at interface and effectively used in removal of drugs [251]. Nayana et al. incorporated Mo2S in CDs results in increased the photocatalytic activity towards degradation of harmful dyes [252]. The material showed strong interaction with electromagnetic waves through the excitations and oscillations of free electrons called plasmons. These interaction results into unique optical phenomena called surface plasmon resonance (SPR). This surface modification results into enhancement in optical and electronic properties of CDs. Li et al. introduced gold nanoparticles into CDs which results into enhancement in their optical properties and used detection of dopamine drug [253].

Wang et al. [169] developed visible light based NCDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst for degradation of indomethacin. The proposed mechanism is shown in Fig. 16a. The electrons-holes (e--h+) pair were generated by photoexcitation of g-C3N4 under visible light wavelength < 460 nm. Further, the NCDs due to the presence of up converted PL properties played important role in converting the light having wavelength > 460 nm into < 460 nm so that more amount of light got absorbed by g-C3N4 material. The NCDs also showed the significant role to maintain sufficient charge separation and catalyse the reaction in an efficient manner. Due the great affinity of NCDs towards electrons, the electrons from conduction band easily transferred to NCDs. From NCDs, oxygen (O2) absorb electrons results in generation of O2 * - radicals which reacted with H+ ions and h+ formed OH* radicals and 1O2 respectively. The generation of reactive species such as O2* -, h+, OH* and 1O2 directly attacked on indomethacin, leading to efficient photocatalytic degradation into product i.e. CO2 and H2O. Lee et al. [254] designed NCDs/TiO2 photocatalyst for reduction of CO2 to methane (CH4). Fig. 16b shows the illumination of light excited the e- in VB to CB and created the (e--h+) pairs in TiO2. Due to excellent storage capacity of NCDs and strong synergy between CDs and TiO2, electrons are quickly transferred to CDs results into significant charge separation. The electrons from CDs transferred to CO2 absorbed over catalyst and have the ability to produce CH4 with aid of protons. However, the holes in VB were captured by water molecules to produce O2 via photooxidation. Shang et al. [255] synthesized ultrathin g-C3N4/NCDs photocatalyst for the photocatalytic hydrogen generation applications. The proposed mechanism illustrates in Fig. 16c, the photo excited electron was generated when CN exposed to visible light with wavelength less than 480 nm. CDs absorbed higher wavelength light and converted it into shorter wavelength so that it becomes suitable for CN absorption. As a result, large amount of (e--h+) pairs were produced and migrated to CDs ensured the charge separation and prevented the recombination charge carriers. Thus NCDs/CN composite displayed enhancement in photocatalytic hydrogen generation performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of CDs with other metal based nano-catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Catalysis played essential role in chemical transformations and environmental remediation applications, with broader prospective in industrial and academic view point [256,257]. Metal based catalyst such as Platinum, Palladium and other costly metals have been dominant because of their high effectiveness. Many metal nanoparticles such as gold and silver have possessed good catalytic abilities, high electrical conductivity. Metal oxide nanoparticles have good chemical stability, catalytic and photocatalytic properties [258,259]. In addition metal oxide based nanoparticles have also been used in photo catalysis, electronics, anti-microbial coating, catalysis, drug delivery, medical imaging, batteries and sensors [260,261]. On the other hand, carbon based nanoparticles including graphene, carbon nanotube have wide range of application because of their high strength and electrical conductivity. However, the excessive amount of metallic catalyst such as cadmium is quite harmful to biological system. The stability of metal based catalyst is highly compromised under high temperature due to their aggregation. Additionally, the high quality metal NPs are very costly. The lung damaging impact of carbon nanotube is well documented in literature [262–264]. However, CDs have possessed several benefits over traditional catalytic materials. CDs are often cheaper due to their ease in preparation from readily available carbon source and waste components. Toxicity factor also played an important role to restrict the use of metallic nanomaterials in pharmaceutical applications. The proper handling and disposal of metallic catalyst further enhanced the processing cost of the system. The risk of leaching into reaction mixture and contamination of products is always remain a crucial factor with metallic nanoparticles during catalytic reactions [265]. Further the deactivation of metallic nanoparticles due to reactant and product poisoning may lose their efficiency with time. To overcome these issues, CDs can be used as alternative to metallic nanoparticles in various catalytic applications [266].

In comparison with the metallic nanomaterials, CDs are often more stable. The rate of agglomeration and oxidation in CDs are comparatively less in contrast to metallic nanoparticles has further supported the higher catalytic activity and efficacy for CDs. The strong carbon-carbon bond and excellent resistance to oxidation further helped CDs in catalysis. The carbon based core of CDs and surface functional group help to keep them stable in all kinds of chemical environment and varied temperature conditions. CDs offer more cost effective solution in catalytic applications. However, metal oxide based nanoparticles have also suffered from sintering issues [267]. The particles have the tendency to get agglomerate and lose their surface area and catalytic activity [268]. The synthesis of metal oxide based nanoparticles often requires highly specific and complex conditions. The catalytic performance of metal oxide based nanoparticles may be affected by surface defects. Moreover, some metal oxides, particularly which contain rare or expensive metals are costly to produce in large scale production [269]. The market analysis of metal based catalyst with per gram price is given in Table 3. However, CDs displayed number of benefits over metal oxide based nanoparticles. In comparison to metal oxide, CDs are less harmful to environment and highly economical. CDs tends to be less cytotoxic and more biocompatible which make them suitable for use in biological system [270]. CDs have intrinsic photoluminescence properties which further enhanced their potential in catalytic applications. A comparative study of CDs based catalyst with other similar nano catalyst shown in Fig. 17.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of CDs with other carbon based nano-catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The other carbon based catalysts such as fullerene, graphene, carbon nanotube, carbon black and activated carbon has specific benefits as well as drawbacks in comparison to CDs. Fullerenes are costly and have lesser number of active sites, which limits its effectiveness [271]. Carbon nanotubes are expensive and require complex procedure for their manufacturing. Carbon nanotubes are quite harmful to inhale. It becomes difficult to spread properly in catalytic system due to dispersion limitations. Also carbon black has small surface area, which affects its catalytic activity. Activated carbon has limited functionalization because of its large surface area which makes it useful for adsorption but it is more rigid. Also activated carbon creates concern about health and environment throughout the production process. However CDs have become better in many kinds of catalytic application over other carbon based catalyst. Compare to other carbon based catalyst; CDs offer significant improvement in catalytic activity. CDs can be easily functionalized with different chemical groups which increase their catalytic efficiency.
